data.title: Winpak's Board of Directors Announces Second Quarter 2026 Dividend
data.description:
WINNIPEG, MB, April 30, 2026 /CNW/ - Winpak Ltd. announces that at a meeting on April 30, 2026, the Company's Board of Directors declared a dividend of five cents (5 cents, Canadian currency) per share. The dividend is payable on July 10, 2026, to shareholders of record at the close of business on July 2, 2026.

Winpak Ltd. manufactures and distributes high-quality packaging materials and related packaging machines. The Company's products are used primarily for the protection of perishable foods and beverages, and in healthcare applications.

SOURCE Winpak Ltd.

/CONTACT:

Copyright CNW Group 2026

data.title: Altria 1Q Sales Rise as Consumers Trade Vapes for Cigarettes — Update
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Altria posted higher sales in the first quarter as its cigarette business performed better than expected.

The company's smokeable business, which includes the Marlboro brand, recorded a slower decline in volumes. Domestic cigarette volumes fell 2.4% compared with the estimated 4% drop across the industry, Chief Executive Billy Gifford told analysts Thursday.

The stock rose to a nine-year high of $73.85 at one point, before trimming those gains to post an advance of 6.6% at $72.70 per share. That pushed shares to 26% gain in the past year.

Gifford attributed the improvement in the cigarette business in part to a crackdown on illegal flavored disposable vape products. The number of consumers who vape has held steady in the past year, and the number of e-vapor consumers declined modestly.

"We've seen some go to nicotine pouch, we've seen some come back to cigarettes," Gifford said of consumers in areas where e-vapes are getting harder to access.

Altria posted a profit of $2.18 billion, or $1.30 a share, in the quarter ended March 31, compared with $1.08 billion, or 63 cents a share, a year earlier.

Stripping out certain one-time items, adjusted per-share earnings were $1.32, ahead of the $1.25 anticipated by analysts, according to FactSet.

Revenue rose 3.2% to $5.43 billion. Analysts surveyed by FactSet forecast revenue of $4.58 billion.

More smokers are trading down to cheaper cigarette brands, as Altria's discount segment gained 2.4 market share points, Gifford said. The pressure from rising prices on fuel related to the Iran war is driving more smokers to trade down, he said.

"You are seeing a growth in the discount category within the cigarette segment, and that is driven by the macroeconomic difficulties that the consumer is facing," Chief Financial Officer Sal Mancuso said.

Management now expects sales growth will be balanced throughout the year, whereas executives were previously expecting the first half of the year to be slower than the second. Like many consumer-facing companies, Altria is keeping a close eye on consumer confidence.

"The strength of the consumer, it's the wild card with the economic outlook the way it is with higher gas prices and stuff," Gifford said.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: PHINIA INC. 1Q 2026: Revenue $878M, EPS $0.96— 10-Q Summary
data.description:
PHINIA INC. reported first-quarter 2026 results with revenue of $878M, net income of $37M and diluted EPS of $0.96, each ahead of the year-ago quarter as the company benefited from higher volumes, favorable FX and its August 2025 SEM acquisition.

Financial Highlights

MetricCurrent quarterPrior year quarterYoY change
Revenue¹$878M$796M10.3%
Net income²$37M$26M42.3%
Diluted EPS³$0.96$0.6352.4%

¹ Reported as “Net sales”. ² Reported as “Net earnings”. ³ Reported as “Earnings per share— diluted”.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue growth of 10% was driven by higher volumes, foreign-exchange gains and the SEM acquisition completed in August 2025.
  • Management shifted the OES business from Aftermarket to Fuel Systems to streamline distribution and reduce administrative complexity.
  • Brand momentum continued across DELPHI®, DELCO REMY® and HARTRIDGE™, with expanded offerings in zero- and low‑carbon fuel technologies following the SEM purchase.
  • Operational improvements from cost controls, tariff recoveries and supplier savings boosted adjusted operating income and margins; the company expects improved earnings and cash generation in 2026 despite softer markets.

Original SEC Filing: PHINIA INC. [ PHIN ] - 10-Q - Apr. 30, 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: Hilton Grand Vacations Inc. 1Q 2026: Revenue $1.29B, EPS $0.79— 10-Q Summary
data.description:
Hilton Grand Vacations Inc. reported first-quarter 2026 results with revenue up to $1.29B and net income of $66M, versus a loss in the prior-year quarter, driven largely by higher VOI sales recognition and growth in real estate and financing revenues.

Financial Highlights

MetricCurrent quarterPrior year quarterYoY change
Revenue¹$1.29B$1.15B11.9%
Net income²$66M($17M)488.2%
Diluted EPS³$0.79($0.17)564.7%

¹ Reported as “Total revenues”. ² Reported as “Net income (loss) attributable to stockholders”. ³ Reported as “Earnings (loss) per share attributable to stockholders”.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Growth: Real estate and financing revenues rose 16.9%, with total revenues up 11.9% year over year driven by higher VOI sales recognition.
  • Channel & Sales Mix: Fee-for-service and just-in-time sales made up 17% and 6% of contract sales, respectively; existing owners accounted for about 74% of sales.
  • Brand & Portfolio Momentum: Continued rebranding of Diamond and Bluegreen properties to Hilton Grand Vacations, supporting a portfolio of more than 200 properties globally.
  • Operational Metrics: Tours increased 8.5% year over year to 189,000, while VPG declined roughly 8.1%, weighing on per‑tour sales despite higher tour flow.
  • Club & Resort Operations: Club membership exceeded 720,000; resort and club revenues were stable with modest expense increases related to employee costs.

Original SEC Filing: Hilton Grand Vacations Inc. [ HGV ] - 10-Q - Apr. 30, 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: Private Indian airports warn government-mandated charge cut may hurt cash flows
data.description:
By Abhijith Ganapavaram

Private Indian airport operators have warned that a government-directed 25% cut in landing and parking charges could strain cash flows and debt servicing, with no enforceable mechanism to ensure that airlines pass on the benefit to passengers, according to a letter seen by Reuters.

  • India's airporttariff regulator ordered major airports to cut landing and parking charges by 25% for three months for domestic flights, following directions from the federal government, to offer relief to the country's airlines that are under financial pressure from the Iran war.

  • The Association of Private Airport Operators (APAO) said in the letter that it supported the intervention but sought urgent policy and regulatory support, citing traffic reductions, revenue losses, cost escalation and operational risks amid uncertainty over the evolving geopolitical situation.

  • The Ministry of Civil Aviation, Airports Authority of India (AAI) and APAO did not immediately respond to Reuters' requests for comment.

  • The order was issued without consultation with airport operators, APAO said, adding that a uniform 25% reduction does not adequately consider airports’ domestic-international traffic mix, creating significant revenue imbalances.

  • The association sought deferment of revenue share or per-passenger fee payments owed to AAI during the relief period and proposed an upward revision in landing and parking charges after the relief period to recover the shortfall.

  • Aviation turbine fuel costs account for a much-larger share of airline costs than landing and parking charges, the letter noted.

  • APAO asked the ministry to ask state governments charging value-added tax on jet fuel above 5% to reduce it to 5% or below.

  • Adani Airports Holdings Ltd, a unit of Adani Enterprises , is India’s largest private airport operator by number of airports, followed by GMR Airports .

data.title: Canada's Economy Returns to Growth, Though Activity Cools in March — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Canada's economy rebounded in the first quarter despite signs activity stalled in March even with a spike in energy prices.

Industry-level gross domestic product rose 0.2% in February, slightly faster than the 0.1% climb recorded in the first month of 2026, and Statistics Canada's advance data indicates output was essentially unchanged in March.

Based on the estimate, the Canadian economy expanded at an annualized pace of 1.7% in the first quarter, after shrinking slightly in final quarter of 2025. That would be a slightly stronger pace than that expected by the Bank of Canada, which anticipates only modest growth this year as the country adjusts to U.S. tariffs that have upended trade.

The data suggests Canada will have twice avoided back-to-back quarterly contractions, the technical definition of recession, in the last year. Yet risks to the outlook remain as the existing North American trade pact comes up for renegotiation and the Iran war drags on and continues to buoy oil prices.

"Headwinds from the energy price shock, U.S. tariffs, trade policy uncertainty and a shrinking population will likely keep recession risks elevated and underlying economic momentum soft in the near term," Michael Davenport, senior economist at Oxford Economics, said.

The national statistics agency said early information for March suggests Canada saw increases in wholesale trade and in transportation and warehousing. That was offset by declines in retail trade and in mining and oil-and-gas extraction. Oil prices continued to climb in March, though a U.S. refinery shutdown during the month may have affected Canadian shipments.

Flat GDP for the latest month would offer a weak handover to the second quarter, though that would be in line with the Bank of Canada's recently updated projections.

The central bank, which this week again left its key interest rate unchanged, forecast growth of 1.5% annualized in both the first and second quarters and 1.2% over the course of the year. The economy contacted 0.6% in the final of last year, due mainly to a sharp drawdown in business inventories that aren't captured in the monthly GDP data.

Statistics Canada's industry accounts for February showed activity was led, for a second straight month, by goods-producing industries, which notched growth of 0.4% on a month earlier versus a 0.1% advance for services industries.

Manufacturing recorded its strongest monthly growth since January 2023, regaining momentum after a rough past year as the industry was hit by tariffs and heightened trade uncertainty. Machinery led the sector's recovery, while auto-industry output was up sharply following contractions in the previous three months, when several assembly plants were shut for model changes and retooling.

Wholesale trade also returned to growth in February, largely offsetting a decline to start the year, and transportation and warehousing saw broad increases and the largest growth in truck transport since March 2021, thanks in part to cross-border goods movement.

Oil-and-gas extraction increased for a second consecutive month, with higher crude-petroleum output in Saskatchewan and Newfoundland and Labrador, as well as a rise in natural gas. A recovery in metal-ore mining supported growth for the sector, coinciding with increased exports of copper.

The public sector, however, dragged on overall activity, with broad-based declines following three monthly increases in a row. The arts, entertainment and recreation industry also contracted in February, the first decline in three months and the largest since January 2022 during the pandemic, dented by a two-week break in the National Hockey League schedule as some players competed in the Winter Olympics.

The country's central bankers remain wary of U.S. trade policy and the potential for an extended war in Iran. For now, policymakers expect consumption and government spending to support the economy even as exports and business investment remain weak and a slowdown in the housing market weighs.

"Canada's economy remained on shaky ground through the first quarter," said Royce Mendes, head of macroeconomic strategy at Desjardins, who views the GDP data as consistent with an economy unlikely to produce excess inflationary pressures. "Should oil prices decline in the coming months, the Bank of Canada can remain on the sidelines, confident that the temporary effects of higher oil prices will not generate a sustained period of high inflation."

As an energy exporter, Canada is set to see a boost in the value of shipments from higher oil prices, but the jump in gasoline prices threatens to squeeze wallets and some businesses. The central bank said there has been little indication so far that higher oil prices have fed through to prices for other goods and services, though the central bank has noted near-term inflation expectations have picked up. Its outlook for the economy assumes oil prices will decline this year.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com
